name: Gangesha Upadhyaya
dates_notes: Mithilā Naiyāyika; dates fixed by Bhattacharya (1958, History of Navya-Nyāya in Mithilā) and Matilal (1968, The Navya-Nyāya Doctrine of Negation) to the late thirteenth and early fourteenth century. The Tattva-Cintāmaṇi's terminus ante quem is given by its citation in Vardhamāna Upādhyāya's Prakāśa.
core_thesis: Gaṅgeśa's *Tattva-Cintāmaṇi* founds Navya-Nyāya — the 'New Logic' — by reformulating the entire *pramāṇa*-machinery of Old-Nyāya in a technical idiom precise enough to defeat the late-twelfth-century Buddhist and Advaitin pressure that Old-Nyāya could no longer absorb. Three innovations define the system. First, *vyāpti* (invariable concomitance) is reformulated. Where Uddyotakara and Udayana relied on a *tri-rūpa-liṅga* characterisation, Gaṅgeśa develops five successively more rigorous candidate-definitions — *anyathāsiddhi-rahita-niyata-pūrva-vṛtti-tva*, *avyabhicarita-sambandha-tva*, and three further refinements — each tested against canonical counter-examples. The standard Navya definition (*pratiyogi-tāvacchedaka-sambandhāvacchinna-pratiyogitāka-bhāva-pratiyogi-tāvacchedaka-rūpeṇa hetu-tāvacchedakāvacchinna-niyamaḥ*) is the form the school inherits. Second, *avacchedaka* (the delimiting condition) is given systematic treatment: every property has its mode of being-a-property, every relation its mode of being-the-relation; this lets every inference and every cognition be specified without remainder. Third, *paryāpti* (the exhausting predication relation) is introduced for cases where a property is located in something via the property's exhaustion of its locus, distinguishing cases where two-ness is in two horses-jointly from cases where horse-ness is in each horse separately. The technical apparatus is the dialectical lingua franca for late-medieval Indian philosophy: Madhusūdana's *Advaita-Siddhi*, Vyāsatīrtha's *Nyāyāmṛta*, Raghunātha Śiromaṇi's *Dīdhiti*, and the entire Navadvīpa scholasticism are unintelligible without it.

engaged_works.title: Tattva-Cintamani
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composition fixed by terminus ante quem of Vardhamāna Upādhyāya's Prakāśa.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; the founding text of Navya-Nyāya.
engaged_works.summary: Independent treatise organised by *pramāṇa* into four *khaṇḍas*: *Pratyakṣa-Khaṇḍa*, *Anumāna-Khaṇḍa*, *Upamāna-Khaṇḍa*, *Śabda-Khaṇḍa*. Each *khaṇḍa* opens with a *maṅgala* establishing the methodological standard, advances through systematic *pūrvapakṣa*-and-*uttarapakṣa* on every disputed sub-question, and concludes with a constructive Navya-Nyāya formulation. The *Anumāna-Khaṇḍa* is the longest and the most influential. Its first section, *Vyāpti-Pañcaka*, gives the five candidate definitions of *vyāpti* and the rigorous tests by which the standard definition is selected; the section on *avacchedaka* establishes the technical idiom that the entire later school presupposes; the section on *paryāpti* introduces the relation that distinguishes joint-locating from distributive-locating predications. The *Pratyakṣa-Khaṇḍa* refines the perceptual analysis to handle Advaitin pressure on the *adhyāsa*-doctrine and Buddhist pressure on *nirvikalpaka* perception. The *Śabda-Khaṇḍa* defends *śābda*-bodha (sentence-cognition) on a Naiyāyika realist basis against the Mīmāṃsā analysis of *anvitābhidhāna*. The *Cintāmaṇi* is the most-commented text in late-medieval Sanskrit philosophy; Raghunātha Śiromaṇi's *Dīdhiti* and Mathurānātha's *Māthurī* are the standard Navadvīpa sub-commentaries.
